narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in this field, and we're here to help you understand the admission decision clearly. The university's model looked at your academic record, including your Year 1 school decile ranking of 1st (well below the typical 6th decile), your Year 3 ranking of 3rd (also under the average 6th decile), your undergraduate GPA of 2.3 (compared to the usual 3.2), your LSAT score of 29 (lower than the average 37), your upper-middle family income, and your plan to attend full-time like most applicants. It predicted a 65% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is the threshold that led to your application's rejection—schools use this to prioritize students likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your low Year 1 decile ranking, as it stood out sharply against what the model sees in students who typically pass. Your undergraduate GPA was the next strongest influence in the same direction, signaling to the model a pattern of academic performance below what's common for bar success. On a more positive note, your full-time enrollment plan helped nudge the prediction toward passing, aligning with the vast majority of successful students. Your upper-middle family income also slightly favored a passing outcome compared to lower brackets in the dataset. Your Year 3 decile and LSAT score had smaller effects, with the former leaning toward failure and the latter slightly toward success, but they weren't as decisive. Overall, your profile compares to typical admitted students by having stronger elements like full-time status and family background, but the early academic deciles and GPA pulled it below the bar-passing threshold. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar starts through targeted effort. Next, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your undergraduate record perhaps through a post-bac program, or gaining relevant experience to demonstrate growth; reapplying with these improvements could shift the model's view significantly. We're rooting for you and encourage reaching out to advisors for personalized strategies. Keep pushing forward—you've got this.
